title: Multi-Tenancy Patterns
summary: Silo vs pool vs bridge models, tenant isolation, data partitioning strategies, and implementing multi-tenancy in Spring Boot.

sections.content:
## What is Multi-Tenancy?

Multi-tenancy is an architecture where a **single software instance serves multiple customers (tenants)**, with each tenant's data isolated from others. Used by SaaS applications.

**Three isolation models:**
- **Silo** — dedicated infrastructure per tenant
- **Pool** — shared infrastructure, logical isolation
- **Bridge** — hybrid (shared compute, isolated storage)

sections.title: Multi-Tenancy Models
sections.content:
graph TB
    subgraph Silo Model
        T1[Tenant A] --> App1[Dedicated App]
        App1 --> DB1[(Dedicated DB)]
        T2[Tenant B] --> App2[Dedicated App]
        App2 --> DB2[(Dedicated DB)]
    end
    subgraph Pool Model
        T3[Tenant A] --> SharedApp[Shared App]
        T4[Tenant B] --> SharedApp
        SharedApp --> SharedDB[(Shared DB\ntenant_id column)]
    end
    subgraph Bridge Model
        T5[Tenant A] --> SharedApp2[Shared App]
        T6[Tenant B] --> SharedApp2
        SharedApp2 --> DB3[(Tenant A DB)]
        SharedApp2 --> DB4[(Tenant B DB)]
    end
    style SharedApp fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style DB3 fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style DB4 fill:#059669,color:#fff

sections.title: Always clear tenant context after each request
sections.content: TenantContext uses ThreadLocal. In thread pool environments, threads are reused. If you don't clear the tenant context after a request, the next request on that thread inherits the previous tenant's context — causing data leakage between tenants. Always use try-finally to guarantee cleanup.

sections.question: In the Pool multi-tenancy model, what is the primary security concern?
options: Higher infrastructure costs from shared resources
Tenant data leakage — a bug in tenant filtering could expose one tenant's data to another
sections.explanation: In the Pool model, all tenants share the same database tables, differentiated only by a tenant_id column. A single bug — forgetting to add a WHERE tenant_id clause, a ThreadLocal leak, a misconfigured Hibernate filter — could expose all tenants' data to any one tenant. This is the critical risk that must be tested exhaustively.
